interview_metadata.interview_location: New York, New York (NAACP National Office vicinity)
interview_metadata.interviewer_name: unknown (likely Julian Bond, based on the interviewer's reference to SNCC as 'my old organization')
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Library of Congress / Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture

main_summary.summary:
Mildred Bond Roxborough recounts her decades-long career on the national staff of the NAACP, which began in 1953 and continued in a full-time role through 1997. Born in Brownsville, Haywood County, Tennessee, she grew up in a household where her parents -- both teachers -- organized one of the early NAACP branches in their county, where Black residents could not vote and outnumbered white residents three to one. As a young child she read The Crisis magazine on her mother's lap, wrote to Roy Wilkins (then the editor of The Crisis) asking permission to sell subscriptions, and received a typed reply granting her permission. She recalls her father being beaten by law-enforcement officials and brought home in 1939, when she was about nine; she describes being told that he was spared jail because the white county coroner, Dr. John Severe, was her grandmother's half-brother and intervened on his behalf.

Roxborough began work at the NAACP National Office in the fall of 1953 under Director of Branches Gloster B. Current, who sent her to Baltimore for a six-week membership campaign under the demanding leadership of branch president Dr. Lillie M. Jackson and Jackson's daughter Juanita Jackson Mitchell. There she came to know branch supporters including Henry G. Parks Jr. of Parks Sausage Company and 'Little Willie' Adams. She offers personal portraits of the NAACP's senior leadership: Thurgood Marshall as a colorful raconteur whose gruffness masked his ability to inspire; Roy Wilkins as an elegant, unpretentious writer and demanding executive who rode the subway to work; Walter White as a sociable figure who 'thrived in the spotlight' and whose ability to 'presume to be Caucasian' allowed him to investigate lynchings in Mississippi, Georgia, and Arkansas without being attacked.

She recounts her field work in Hoxie, Arkansas (her first major assignment, where she walked desegregating Black children to school), her work with Daisy Bates in Arkansas during the lead-up to Little Rock Central High integration, her meetings with Rosa Parks in Montgomery (where she stayed in the Parks home before the bus boycott), and her work with E.D. Nixon. She describes the office reaction to Emmett Till's 1955 murder, coming shortly after the killing of NAACP branch president Gus Courts in Mississippi, and her work coordinating a national speaking tour for Emmett's mother Mamie Till Bradley. She narrates the case of Clarence Norris, the last surviving Scottsboro defendant, who walked into the NAACP office seeking help; she relates how General Counsel Nathaniel R. Jones assigned attorney James I. Meyerson to the case and Norris ultimately received a full pardon in Alabama.

She describes her travels with Medgar Evers in the Mississippi Delta interviewing farmers denied credit because of NAACP activity -- famously discovering her bare feet were resting on his shotgun in the car -- and her work staying in NAACP families' homes across the South. She comments on the succession of NAACP executive leadership (Walter White to Roy Wilkins in 1955; Wilkins to Benjamin L. Hooks), discusses the founding of the ACT-SO youth-achievement program by Benjamin L. Hooks and Vernon Jarrett, and reflects on the NAACP's relationship to peer organizations (SCLC, SNCC, CORE, the Black Panther Party). She offers an extended argument that the NAACP's record of legislative lobbying -- including its role in the campaign that led to FDR's Executive Order 8802 establishing the Fair Employment Practices Committee -- is under-recognized relative to its courtroom victories. She closes with reflections on women's contributions to the NAACP (Daisy Lampkin, Lucille Black, Bobbie Branch, Althea T.L. Simmons, Constance Baker Motley, Frances Hooks, Margaret Bush Wilson, Roslyn M. Brock), her brief written exchange with Dr. W.E.B. Du Bois, and her own composure under fear during dangerous Southern travel.
key_themes: NAACP Institutional History and Organizational Continuity
Generational Transmission of Civil Rights Activism in a Tennessee Family
NAACP Field Work Under Conditions of Southern Repression
Personal Portraits of NAACP National Leaders (Marshall, Wilkins, White, Hooks)
Legislative Lobbying vs. Litigation as Civil Rights Strategy
Mentorship and Youth Programs (ACT-SO, Youth Councils)
main_summary.historical_significance: Roxborough's testimony is a rare, comprehensive insider account of the NAACP National Office from 1953 through the late 1990s, spanning the Walter White, Roy Wilkins, and Benjamin L. Hooks executive eras. As one of the longest-tenured staff members in NAACP national history and one of the few women in mid-century executive positions there, she provides first-person witness on the NAACP's day-to-day operations, the personalities of its leadership, and its under-documented legislative-lobbying work. Her direct field work with Medgar Evers in the Mississippi Delta, with Daisy Bates in Arkansas, and her coordination of the post-murder national tour for Mamie Till Bradley document organizational responses to the most consequential events of the Movement era, contributing important context to a historical record that has predominantly centered the NAACP's legal department over its branch and program operations.

errors: The summary names 'Roy Wilkins' as 'editor of The Crisis' at the moment Roxborough wrote to him as a child. Roxborough does describe Wilkins as 'the editor of The Crisis magazine' in the transcript (segments 41, 45-46). However, the ground-truth corpus establishes that Wilkins took over The Crisis from W.E.B. Du Bois in 1934 (matching Roxborough's account). This is therefore correctly transcript-supported; no error.

The summary calls Mamie Till Bradley by that name, which is consistent with the transcript (segment 113). Note: she later remarried and became Mamie Till-Mobley; the transcript-period name is correct.

The summary refers to Roxborough's 1939 account of being 'about nine' when her father was beaten (segments 49-53). The transcript's '1939' framing was given by the interviewer in segment 48; Roxborough confirms ('Oh, well, by 1939...') and gives her age as 'about nine at the time' in segment 53. No error.

Charles Evers — the transcript at segment 315 says her brother 'eventually became mayor of Philadelphia, I believe it was Charles.' The ground-truth corpus establishes Charles Evers became mayor of Fayette, Mississippi in 1969, not Philadelphia, Mississippi. The summary correctly attributes the speaker's framing of 'her brother took the position' of Mississippi field director without repeating the speaker's apparent geographic confusion, so no claim error is introduced into the summary.
improvements: Consider adding the exact date framing of Walter White's death (1955) when discussing the Wilkins succession, which the transcript supports in segment 206.

The main summary could explicitly note that the interview's discussion of the 'banning' of the NAACP in Alabama refers to the period roughly 1956-1964, which the interviewer references obliquely; the transcript supports this contextual addition only implicitly so it was left out to avoid unsupported framing.
The summary could quote Roxborough's striking metaphor of being 'baptized' in the Baltimore branch experience (segment 67) to better preserve the speaker's voice.

chapters.title: Childhood in Brownsville, Tennessee: Voting Rights and the Founding of the Local NAACP
chapters.summary: Roxborough opens with the Library of Congress and Smithsonian framing introduction. She describes growing up in Haywood County, Tennessee, one of three Tennessee counties where Black residents could not vote despite outnumbering whites three to one. Her parents, both teachers, campaigned to get Black residents to register to vote at the county courthouse in Brownsville, the county seat. They believed organizing the Black community to exercise political power was the path to changing the quality of life, and this work predated their formal organizing of a local NAACP branch.

improvements: Could quote Roxborough's exact phrasing that her parents wanted to 'marshal the black citizens in greater strength' (segment 21) to preserve her voice.

chapters.title: Walter White, The Crisis Magazine, and Writing to Roy Wilkins
chapters.summary: Roxborough describes her parents' formal organizing of an NAACP branch, including their need for a charter from Walter White, then executive secretary of the NAACP. At about age seven she was reading The Crisis magazine, to which her parents subscribed, and her mother read poetry from it -- including Langston Hughes -- to her at bedtime. When she was nine years old she wrote to Roy Wilkins, then editor of The Crisis, asking permission to sell subscriptions. He sent back a typed letter with his signature granting permission, of which Roxborough says she was 'beside myself' proud.

improvements: Could add the detail that Roxborough specified she would sell subscriptions through her own means but her mother insisted she write the letter herself (segment 42).

chapters.title: 1939: Her Father Beaten for Civil Rights Activity
chapters.summary: Roxborough recounts that by 1939 her parents had organized the local NAACP branch, with her father as president and mother as secretary, and her father had been arrested multiple times. One evening law-enforcement officials brought him home badly beaten, telling Roxborough -- then nine years old and home alone -- to 'take care of your paw' and noting that 'if he did not have connections, he would be in jail.' Her mother later explained that Dr. John Severe, the white county coroner of Haywood County and her grandmother's half-brother, intervened to spare her father further punishment because he was 'widely respected' in the county.

improvements: Could include the speaker's emotional framing that she was 'of course, frightened' (segment 53).

chapters.title: Howard University During World War II and Segregation in Washington
chapters.summary: Roxborough describes attending Howard University from 1943 to 1945 during World War II, and later attending the Washington Square campus of New York University. At Howard, she and other students went to the Howard Theater because other Washington theaters were segregated. Students discussed segregation and sometimes individually tested restaurant exclusion, but the issue did not escalate to organized protests during her time there. The campus was filled with uniformed servicemen as part of federal wartime training programs.

improvements: Could specify Roxborough's framing that she was 'concerned about our studies' alongside segregation (segment 60).

chapters.title: Joining NAACP Staff (1953): Baltimore, Lillie M. Jackson, and Henry G. Parks Jr.
chapters.summary: Roxborough joined the NAACP National Office staff in the fall of 1953 to do a special project for Gloster B. Current, Director of Branches. Current sent her to Baltimore -- 'one of our largest branches in the country' -- for a six-week membership campaign under the leadership of branch president Dr. Lillie M. Jackson, who was 'colorful and very strong and aggressive' and 'difficult to please.' Thurgood Marshall laughed at her assignment, telling Current 'why are you doing that to that girl?' Working alongside Jackson's daughter Juanita Jackson Mitchell, then organizer of the National NAACP Youth Program, Roxborough successfully ran the campaign. She names two key Baltimore supporters: Henry G. Parks Jr., president of Parks Sausage Company (an early Black corporation, 'if not the first of its kind, was one of the first'), and 'Little Willie' Adams, locally known as a numbers operator. She describes the two as occupying 'extremes' that nevertheless came together to support the NAACP cause.

improvements: Could quote her saying she was 'baptized in that particular branch experience' (segment 69).

chapters.title: Personalities of the NAACP National Office: Thurgood Marshall, Roy Wilkins, and Walter White
chapters.summary: Roxborough offers personal portraits of three senior NAACP leaders. Thurgood Marshall used 'so many cuss words' but was 'a wonderful raconteur' with a great sense of humor who 'was also very effective in getting you to do things and making difficult subjects sound easy.' Roy Wilkins she describes as 'very elegant,' concerned with language and syntax (she learned 'much about grammar and composition' from him), unpretentious -- he rode the five-cent subway to work and refused bodyguards -- but a 'purist' about staff dedication and proper presentation of the NAACP. Walter White she portrays as 'colorful' and someone who 'thrived in the spotlight,' contrasting him with Wilkins who 'tolerated' the spotlight. White was so fair-skinned he could 'presume to be Caucasian,' enabling him to investigate lynchings in Mississippi, Georgia, and Arkansas without being attacked.

improvements: Could explicitly note the speaker's characterization of White's racial passing as 'effective' for investigative purposes (segment 86).

chapters.title: Daisy Bates, Rosa Parks, and E.D. Nixon
chapters.summary: Roxborough offers portraits of three Movement figures she encountered in the field. Daisy Bates she met first in Hoxie, Arkansas in 1954 and again two or three years later during the lead-up to the integration of Little Rock Central High, when Orval Faubus was governor. Bates was 'colorful, aggressive, didn't mind the spotlight, and very talkative' and was able to bring branches and 'the little people' -- many 'barely literate' -- into NAACP work focused on education. Rosa Parks, whom Roxborough first met in Montgomery before the bus boycott (Roxborough stayed in the Parks home), she describes as 'laconic' -- of few words but listened to when she spoke -- and 'quietly militant' with her husband; Parks was then secretary of the Montgomery branch and was focused on youth organizing. Roxborough briefly characterizes E.D. Nixon as 'a very dedicated person with a strong commitment to civil rights' who 'refused to give up the membership rolls of the NAACP in Alabama when the Attorney General demanded it' -- after which the NAACP was banned from operating in Alabama.

improvements: Could note that the transcript's '1955 — I'm sorry, 1954' (segment 90) shows Roxborough self-correcting on the year of her first Little Rock visit, which the chapter summary should preserve for fidelity.

chapters.title: The Murder of Emmett Till and the National Tour with Mamie Till Bradley
chapters.summary: Roxborough recounts the NAACP National Office reaction to the 1955 murder of Emmett Till in Mississippi, framing it as 'a great tragedy' that came shortly after the killing of branch president Gus Courts (whom Roxborough struggles to recall the town of) in Mississippi. The office felt that 'Mississippi was just completely going out of bounds' and that the NAACP had to 'stay behind its people to help them and to keep them from having to be cave in.' She describes arranging and partially accompanying a national NAACP-branch tour for Mamie Till Bradley, Emmett's mother, who grew 'more militant and more informed' and 'made demands' for justice for her son as the tour progressed. She references Bradley's insistence on the open casket at the Chicago funeral 'showing the evidences of his having been killed.'

improvements: Could note that the speaker self-identifies hesitation about the Mississippi town where Courts was killed (segment 107).

chapters.title: Clarence Norris of the Scottsboro Boys Walks Into the NAACP Office
chapters.summary: Roxborough narrates the late-1960s walk-in of Clarence Norris, the last of the Scottsboro defendants, at the NAACP's 1790 Broadway office. After Norris was greeted by office manager Bobbie Branch (who had met him decades earlier at the original 20 West Florida Street office), he met with Dr. John Morsell, then Assistant to Roy Wilkins. Norris's first request was for financial assistance for his ill wife, but he eventually said 'I want to clear my name before I die.' General Counsel Nathaniel R. Jones assigned attorney James I. Meyerson to pursue a pardon. Meyerson, Wilkins, Jones, and Norris ultimately traveled to Alabama, met with the governor and attorney general, and Norris received a full pardon. Roxborough notes the historical context: the NAACP had been involved in the original Scottsboro defense but stepped aside when the Communist Party became involved.

errors: Transcript inconsistently uses both 'Willie Norris' (segments 150, 173) and 'Clarence Norris' (segment 124). Historical record establishes Clarence Norris as the canonical name; 'Willie' appears to be a transcript or memory artifact and is not used in the summary.
improvements: Could include the speaker's framing that Norris was 'a fugitive' (segment 129) walking in voluntarily.

chapters.title: The ACT-SO Youth Achievement Program
chapters.summary: Roxborough clarifies that she was never Director of Education (June Shagaloff held that role) but worked in many roles, eventually becoming Director of Programs, under which Education fell. She describes the founding of ACT-SO -- the Afro-Academic, Cultural, Technological and Scientific Olympics -- by Benjamin L. Hooks and Chicago newspaper reporter Vernon Jarrett. ACT-SO recruited promising youth in 24 academic, cultural, scientific, arts, and literary fields, with local, regional, and national competitions. Roxborough states that as of the time of the interview there were some 600 ACT-SO units across the country, with the Metropolitan New York unit being the largest at 17 participating branches -- 'probably the largest African-American talent-based scholarship program in the country.'

improvements: Could note the speaker declined to name specific ACT-SO alumni to 'avoid leaving some out' (segment 199).

chapters.title: NAACP Executive Succession: Walter White, Roy Wilkins, and Benjamin L. Hooks
chapters.summary: Roxborough confirms Walter White died in 1955 while on leave (he had taken multiple leaves of absence in his last years; Roy Wilkins had been serving as administrator). After the spring board meeting following White's death, the Board named Wilkins his successor. She offers a portrait of Benjamin L. Hooks (who succeeded Wilkins) as a 'Baptist minister' with 'great oratorical skills' who used 'the gospel approach' to inspire NAACP constituents, contrasting with Wilkins's 'intellectual' style and 'elegant prose.' Hooks traveled extensively to maintain the connection between the National Office and the NAACP's 1,700 units across all 50 states plus 500 youth and college units. Roxborough then reflects on the apparent gap between the NAACP's 100-plus-year record of achievement and the public's limited awareness of it, attributing this to a broader tendency among Americans (Black and otherwise) to 'forget our history.'

improvements: Could explicitly reference Boynton v. Virginia (1960), which the speaker names in segment 230, as a representative landmark of NAACP-affected jurisprudence.

chapters.title: The NAACP and Its Peer Organizations: SCLC, SNCC, CORE, Black Panthers
chapters.summary: The interviewer asks Roxborough to comment on Movement-era peer organizations. SCLC she calls 'effective' for bringing religious leaders into the public face of civil rights and for flexibly mobilizing local congregations around specific causes. SNCC -- which she notes the interviewer was part of -- she describes as having 'opened the eyes of the o-fokes,' with sit-ins demonstrating to older adults that 'this is something that all of us could do.' CORE she calls 'a little more intellectual,' with 'an excellent leader in James Farmer who formerly worked for the NAACP as our program director'; CORE was 'more integrated than the other groups' and 'appealed to a younger group.' The Black Panther Party she critiques as having 'a sense of misdirection' that began as a militant group wanting to apply power actively but 'veered toward separation and separatism,' which she considers 'self-defeating.' She also notes that 'so many of these leaders by the way of the other organizations had gotten their grounding and their foundations in the NAACP, such as Martin Luther King did, Jr. and his father had been active in the NAACP before him.'

improvements: Note the speaker says James Farmer 'formerly worked for the NAACP as our program director'; ground-truth corpus establishes Farmer was at the Fellowship of Reconciliation before founding CORE, but the NAACP staff role is also documented; preserved as stated.

chapters.title: Legal Strategy vs. Lobbying: The NAACP's Under-Recognized Legislative Work
chapters.summary: Roxborough disputes the framing of the NAACP as 'primarily a legal organization.' She points to FDR's Executive Order 8802 (the Fair Employment Practices Committee preliminary order) as the product of NAACP lobbying, facilitated by Eleanor Roosevelt's role on the NAACP Board of Directors and Walter White's relationship with her -- White, Eleanor Roosevelt, and A. Philip Randolph together secured the executive order desegregating federal installations. The first Civil Rights Law, she says, was secured primarily through the Washington Bureau's lobbying work under Clarence M. Mitchell Jr. She attributes public misperception to the visibility of Supreme Court victories -- General Counsel Robert L. Carter made 'some 36 appearances' before the Supreme Court winning 'about 30,' and Thurgood Marshall before becoming Justice argued 'more than two dozen times' -- which made legal action the public face of NAACP achievement.

errors: The transcript phrase 'Form of General Counsel Robert Carter, it made some 36 appearances' (segment 264) is grammatically ambiguous in the Whisper rendering -- it appears to mean 'Former General Counsel Robert Carter.' The ground-truth corpus confirms Robert L. Carter was NAACP General Counsel (1956-1968) and did argue substantially before the Supreme Court (the corpus credits him with arguing McLaurin v. Oklahoma State Regents and other cases, though does not give a precise lifetime SCOTUS count). The summary preserves the speaker's '36 appearances/won about 30' as a speaker claim, not a fact; the ground-truth corpus does not contradict it.
improvements: Could quote Roxborough's exact phrasing about Thurgood Marshall: 'before he became a Justice when he was NAACP Counsel, appeared before the Supreme Court...more than two dozen times' (segment 265).

chapters.title: Malcolm X and His Impact on NAACP Youth
chapters.summary: Asked about Malcolm X's effect on the NAACP, Roxborough describes him as having 'demonstrated the fact that discipline could be achieved in leadership.' His followers were 'in so many instances, young men who had been street people' who became part of 'a cohesive group' and projected discipline back onto the community. She says his death had 'a serious impact' because it ended a leadership of such 'vast experience' over previously unorganized populations. Within the NAACP, she observed especially among 'our younger adults and youth' a sense that Malcolm 'had the right idea in terms of his discipline,' particularly as his rhetoric 'became more bland before he died than it was when he began' and he modified his position toward 'developing pressure on the power structure.'

improvements: Could quote Roxborough's striking phrase that Malcolm 'was preaching almost anarchy at one point in time' (segment 275).

chapters.title: Marriage to John Roxborough and the Detroit Branch
chapters.summary: Roxborough met her future husband John, an attorney born and raised in Michigan, when he was Chairman of the Legal Redress Committee of the Detroit NAACP branch. She was in Detroit working with the local branch (under Arthur L. Johnson) organizing the first Freedom Fund Life Membership dinner; the Legal Redress Committee under John had won two or three cases in the Michigan Supreme Court involving housing and education. The committee's work received commendation from Thurgood Marshall at the National Office. She says the Detroit branch represented the model of 'kind of activities and accomplishments that an NAACP branch should undertake,' and notes that their shared civil rights interest was 'one of the reasons of the first, the common interest in the early days' of their relationship.

chapters.title: The NAACP Banned in Alabama, Field Work with Medgar Evers, and the Mississippi Delta
chapters.summary: Roxborough describes the National Office's defiant response to Alabama's demand for NAACP membership rolls: 'We are not going to give those membership lists up.' She recently had hired Ruby Hurley to organize Southeast Region NAACP units from Birmingham; after the Alabama ban Hurley relocated her office to Atlanta and continued 'almost surreptitiously' organizing in Alabama, Mississippi, and elsewhere. After Medgar Evers's assassination, the National Office sent staff to Mississippi to 'fill the void' and Evers's brother Charles took over the field secretary role. Roxborough then narrates her own field work with Medgar Evers in the Mississippi Delta in the 1960s, interviewing farmers denied credit for the winter because of NAACP activity. She tells the story of being in Evers's car at night with her shoes off and feeling something cold underfoot -- 'that's my shotgun, you have your feet on' -- and of nighttime drives between Yazoo and Mound Bayou, Mississippi (a Black town with a motel where they could stay).

errors: Transcript at segment 315 says Charles Evers 'eventually became mayor of Philadelphia, I believe it was Charles.' Ground-truth corpus establishes Charles Evers became mayor of Fayette, Mississippi in 1969, not Philadelphia, Mississippi. The chapter summary attributes the field-director succession to Charles Evers (confirmed by ground truth) without repeating the speaker's mistaken city attribution.
improvements: Could explicitly cite the shotgun moment (segments 326-328) as a representative anecdote of the danger of the work.

chapters.title: The Little Rock Nine: Ernest Green and Minnijean Brown
chapters.summary: Following a tape change, Roxborough returns to the Little Rock Nine, whom she met as a group through Daisy Bates rather than as individuals (Bates carefully protected them for security). Roxborough specifically remembers Ernest Green for his 'quiet determination' -- Green later became an NAACP Board of Directors member -- and Minnijean Brown for her 'special, sturdy personality.' The other students she characterizes as more quietly engaged but uniformly determined to complete their schooling. She notes the year framing: the year before Central High's formal desegregation, then the actual integration the next fall.

improvements: Whisper transcript renders Brown's personality as 'special 30 personality' (segment 355) — appears to be a misrendering of 'sturdy personality'; the chapter summary uses the contextually-clear word.

chapters.title: Women in the NAACP: Ella Baker, Daisy Lampkin, Constance Baker Motley, and the Women's Founders
chapters.summary: Asked about Septima Poinsette Clark and Bernice Robinson, Roxborough says she met them but knew them mainly by reputation. She offers more on Ella Baker, who worked at the NAACP Department of Branches before Roxborough joined and later became 'focused on our youth' and 'actually was involved in the founding of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee.' Baker was 'very forceful' and aggressive. Roxborough then offers a broader account of women in the NAACP, noting that at her start most women were in support/secretary roles. She names Daisy E. Lampkin (a Pittsburgh National Board member traveling the country in the 1930s-1940s garnering memberships); Lucille Black (entered at age 17 as a secretary, rose to Membership Secretary); Bobbie Branch (entered as a clerk, became Office Manager); June Shagaloff (field director in education); Constance Baker Motley (Legal Department, later head of the NAACP Legal Defense Fund); Althea T.L. Simmons (succeeded Clarence M. Mitchell Jr. as head of the Washington Bureau); Frances Hooks (wife of Benjamin Hooks, founder of Women in the NAACP); Margaret Bush Wilson (Chair of the Board); and Roslyn M. Brock (Vice Chair, who succeeded as Chair). She notes the foundational truth that 'without the women, we wouldn't have an NAACP' and recounts that the 1918 lynching-investigation report that triggered the 1919 silent March in New York was 'done by a female' who submitted it through the Washington Bureau but was publicly overshadowed by Dr. W.E.B. Du Bois.

errors: The transcript at segment 419 contains a Whisper artifact reading 'You wound up having Margaret Bush Wilson + Roslyn M. Brock become the second female to be chairman of the board of the NAACP' -- the speaker apparently means that Margaret Bush Wilson became the first female board chair, and Roslyn Brock the second. The chapter summary preserves both figures' roles as documented across the surrounding segments (419-420), avoiding overcommitting to a specific ordinal that the Whisper rendering muddles. Note the 1919 'silent March' attribution: the NAACP's canonical 1917 Silent Parade was led by W.E.B. Du Bois, James Weldon Johnson, and others — Roxborough names the year as 1919 (the parade is historically dated to July 28, 1917). This is flagged as a transcript chronology slip that the summary preserves as spoken rather than 'correcting,' to remain faithful to the speaker.
improvements: Could clarify that the Silent March/Silent Parade Roxborough describes is historically dated 1917, not 1918-1919; the summary preserves the speaker's framing rather than overriding it.

chapters.title: Brief Contact with W.E.B. Du Bois and Reflections on Her NAACP Career
chapters.summary: Roxborough recounts that Dr. W.E.B. Du Bois was 'even before my time' but she once wrote him for a copy of one of his papers; he replied that he would be 'delighted to send it' for five dollars, which she paid. She declines to share secondhand Du Bois stories she heard from others 'so I won't undertake to do that and mislead the history.' Asked what she would like the public to know about her work, she demurs, saying her satisfaction came from helping NAACP leaders in her 'small way' rather than from public recognition. She describes a five-year rotation pattern through 'almost every department in the association except its accounting department' -- field secretary at large, executive assistant to the executive secretary, director of operations, director of programs, and eventually development director (for which she took fundraising coursework). She reflects on the unusual position of being 'a young, unattached female' traveling alone in the early 1950s into both Northern and Deep Southern branch contexts and the diplomacy and self-protection she had to learn.

improvements: Could explicitly note her self-conscious refusal to repeat secondhand Du Bois stories as an indication of historical-care discipline.

chapters.title: Fear, Danger, and the NAACP's Meaning in Communities
chapters.summary: Asked whether she ever felt fear in the field, Roxborough describes a recurring pattern: 'my reaction of fear has come after I've gotten through it because you don't have time to be afraid.' She recounts traveling at night across cotton fields to reach local meetings in Hoxie, Arkansas, El Dorado and Cotton Plant in Arkansas, and the Mississippi Delta with Medgar Evers, including the shotgun moment, while patrolled by police. Her parents had taught her 'you're an idiot if you get afraid while you're doing something, because you've become your own worst enemy.' She estimates she worked in 'about 40 states' during her five-year field secretary period. She closes by reflecting on the danger she sometimes brought to families who housed her in small Southern towns -- 'nothing's secret eventually that town knows what the folks are doing' -- and on the NAACP's enduring meaning in communities where 'people have felt that they could get help going to the NAACP, that there would be something done in their behalf.' The interview ends with the standard Library of Congress / Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture closing.
related_events: NAACP branches in the Deep South

improvements: Could quote Roxborough's parental teaching verbatim (segment 480) for narrative impact.

interview_metadata.interview_location: New York, New York (NAACP National Office vicinity)
interview_metadata.interviewer_affiliation: Library of Congress / Smithsonian National Museum of African American History and Culture

evidence: Strong opening establishing Brownsville TN voting context and family activism (Segment 12, 00:00:50,000)
Logical move through joining NAACP in 1953 to Baltimore assignment to executive portraits (Segments 61-86, 00:09:50,000 - 00:16:54,000)
Interviewer-driven structure: rapid topic-shifting through NAACP personalities, then organizational comparisons, then biographical wrap (Segments 234-280, 00:38:48,000 - 00:50:35,000)
story_structure_coherence.justification: Interview has a strong thematic arc -- family origins to NAACP staff career to comparative organizational reflection to personal legacy -- but transitions are interviewer-driven rather than narrator-driven. Roxborough's responses are organized but sometimes hop between organizations/people as the questions move. Final reflection on fear and NAACP meaning provides satisfying narrative resolution.

most_vivid_moment.quote: I said to them, what is this kid, maybe I can move it. He said, well, that's my shotgun, you have your feet on. So of course my feet flew up.

evidence: Specific 1939 beating account with intervening county coroner Dr. John Severe (Segments 49-55, 00:05:54,000)
Father's quote from white officials: 'Take care of your paw. He's been hurt' (Segment 52, 00:06:43,000)
Thurgood Marshall's quote: 'Gloucester, why are you doing that to that girl? You should be ashamed of yourself' (Segment 66, 00:11:02,000)
Shotgun moment in Medgar Evers's car at night in Mississippi Delta (Segment 327, 00:59:28,000)
Du Bois charging her five dollars for a copy of his paper (Segment 429, 01:14:06,000)
vivid_details_examples.justification: Roxborough provides exceptional specificity for personalities -- multiple direct quotes from Marshall, Wilkins, Bates, and others, with first/last names and clear dates throughout. The shotgun moment and her father's beating are vivid and 'you-are-there' scenes. Some passages of organizational commentary are more abstract.

most_authentic_moment.quote: And I was so proud to get the letter from the editor of The Crisis magazine. I was beside myself.

vulnerability_elements: Acknowledges fear of being a nine-year-old alone with her beaten father (Segment 53)
Admits 'how could you get in such a situation, out in the middle of nowhere and nobody on whom to call' (Segment 477)
Demurs from publicly claiming her own accomplishments ('I don't... I'm most proud of this or that or the other,' Segment 464)
Refuses to repeat secondhand Du Bois stories 'to mislead the history' (Segment 433)
emotional_authenticity.justification: Roxborough's emotional access is consistent but measured -- she expresses pride, fear, and humility authentically but maintains a degree of professional reserve characteristic of an executive of her generation. Most emotional disclosure comes through specific anecdotes (the beating, the shotgun, the Crisis letter) rather than direct emotional discussion.

narrative_quality.overall_assessment: Roxborough delivers strong narrative quality through specific personalities, dated events, and memorable scenes (shotgun, father's beating, Du Bois charging five dollars). Her composure reflects an executive's communication style: organized, factually grounded, and reserved in self-disclosure. The interviewer's frequent topic shifts limit her ability to develop extended narrative arcs.

major_events: Joining NAACP National staff at the moment of Brown v. Board and just before Emmett Till (Segment 61, 00:09:50,000)
First-person witness to Walter White / Roy Wilkins / Benjamin Hooks executive transitions (Segments 81-86, 202-216)
Personal participation in Hoxie AR desegregation 1954 and Little Rock Nine lead-up (Segments 90, 350-361)
Field work alongside Medgar Evers in Mississippi Delta interviewing economically-reprised farmers (Segments 318-330)
Coordinator and partial accompanist of national speaking tour for Mamie Till Bradley (Segments 114-119)
Direct involvement in the Clarence Norris NAACP pardon case (Segments 122-176)

key_figures.name: Walter White
key_figures.relationship: NAACP Executive Secretary at her hire

key_figures.name: Roy Wilkins
key_figures.relationship: Long-time supervisor and editor she wrote to as a child

key_figures.name: Thurgood Marshall
key_figures.relationship: Colleague in early NAACP staff years

key_figures.name: Benjamin L. Hooks
key_figures.relationship: Executive Director under whom she served

key_figures.name: Rosa Parks
key_figures.relationship: NAACP branch secretary whose home she stayed in

organizational_involvement.role: Field secretary, executive assistant, director of operations, director of programs, development director (rotated every ~5 years)

unique_firsthand_perspective.unique_access: She had longitudinal insider access to NAACP National Office across three executive eras (White, Wilkins, Hooks), the Mississippi Delta voter-registration work, the Little Rock crisis, the Scottsboro/Norris pardon case, and women's leadership trajectory in the NAACP. Few surviving witnesses combine her span and breadth.
unique_firsthand_perspective.justification: Roxborough's vantage is exceptional: 44 years on the national staff of the country's foundational civil rights organization, with first-person work alongside virtually every major NAACP figure of the period. Her perspective on legislative-lobbying versus litigation is informed insider knowledge unavailable in published sources.

precision_examples: 1939 father beating with intervening Dr. John Severe (Segments 49-55)
Wilkins succession at the spring 1955 board meeting after Walter White's death (Segments 206, 211)
Detroit branch Michigan Supreme Court cases in housing and education (Segment 281)
Robert L. Carter's 36 Supreme Court appearances with ~30 wins (Segment 264)
verifiable_claims: NAACP General Counsel Nathaniel R. Jones assigned James I. Meyerson to Clarence Norris's pardon case
ACT-SO co-founded by Benjamin L. Hooks and Vernon Jarrett
Mamie Till Bradley national branch tour coordinated through NAACP
Henry G. Parks Jr. of Parks Sausage Company as Baltimore branch supporter
Ruby Hurley led NAACP Southeast Region from Birmingham, relocated to Atlanta after Alabama ban
Charles Evers succeeded Medgar Evers as Mississippi field director
specificity_of_details.justification: Roxborough offers historian-grade precision on personnel, organizational structure, and event sequences -- many claims (Robert Carter's SCOTUS appearance count, ACT-SO founding, Norris pardon attorneys) are independently verifiable. A few dates are approximate or self-corrected, and one geographic claim (Charles Evers as mayor of 'Philadelphia') is mistaken.

new_information: Detailed account of women's leadership pipeline within NAACP National Office (Lampkin, Black, Branch, Shagaloff, Motley, Simmons, Hooks, Wilson, Brock)
Internal NAACP perception of the Walter White / Roy Wilkins / Benjamin Hooks personalities, not in published institutional histories
First-person account of the Clarence Norris pardon initiative (1970s)
Insider account of the Mamie Till Bradley national NAACP-branch tour and her growing militancy
Specific Baltimore branch supporters (Henry G. Parks Jr., 'Little Willie' Adams)
Personal dynamic of being a young unattached woman traveling alone in the Deep South in the early 1950s
understudied_aspects: Daily operations of NAACP National Office across multiple executive eras
Women's professional advancement within the NAACP staff hierarchy
NAACP's legislative-lobbying work as distinct from courtroom litigation
Field-work logistics in clandestine post-Alabama-ban organizing
fills_historical_gaps.narrative_complications: Challenges the popular framing of the NAACP as 'primarily legal' by detailing its lobbying contributions (EO 8802 / FEPC, Civil Rights Act of 1957). Complicates Big Six narratives by highlighting women's foundational role.
fills_historical_gaps.historiographical_value: Indispensable insider source for NAACP institutional history; oral counterpart to Gloster B. Current's, Roy Wilkins's, and Benjamin Hooks's memoirs. Particularly valuable for women's-history scholars of the Movement.
fills_historical_gaps.justification: Roxborough's interview makes substantial new contributions to NAACP institutional history, particularly on women's advancement and the legislative-lobbying dimension. The Clarence Norris pardon narrative is documented but Roxborough's first-person walk-in account adds specificity.

historical_value.overall_assessment: This is among the most historiographically valuable NAACP interviews in the Civil Rights History Project. Roxborough's 44-year span, her presence at multiple inflection points (1955 Wilkins succession, Mississippi Delta field work, Norris pardon, Hooks succession), and her self-discipline about not embellishing secondhand stories make her testimony unusually trustworthy and dense with verifiable detail.

transformation_growth.transformation_arc: Roxborough traces a journey from nine-year-old subscription seller for The Crisis to elderly NAACP development director, framing this as a gradual professional rotation through 'almost every department' rather than a single before/after moment.
key_turning_points: 1939 father's beating and learning of family kinship across the color line (Segments 49-55, 00:05:54,000)
1953 'baptism' in the Baltimore branch under Dr. Lillie M. Jackson (Segments 67-69, 00:11:12,000)
Field-work years 1953-1958: learning diplomacy and self-protection as young unattached woman (Segments 444-452, 01:16:34,000)
Mid-career rotation into development and fundraising professional certification (Segments 458-461)
transformation_growth.before_after_contrast: She moved from being a 'frightened' nine-year-old hearing of her father's beating to a seasoned operations executive who taught herself fundraising at university 'so I would know the technical side.' Her self-presentation is one of measured competence rather than dramatic transformation.
transformation_growth.inspirational_quality: Inspirational primarily through her demonstration of decades-long institutional loyalty and willingness to keep learning. The interview models the value of skilled professional service in a movement context.
transformation_growth.justification: Roxborough discusses growth as professional development rather than dramatic transformation -- a measured and authentic arc, but less inspirationally pitched than testimonies built around conversion or breakthrough moments. The Baltimore 'baptism' and her parents' advice on fear ('you're an idiot if you get afraid') are the closest moments to transformative articulation.

peak_moments.stakes: Father's life; nine-year-old daughter alone with him

peak_moments.description: Shotgun-underfoot in Medgar Evers's car at night in Mississippi Delta
peak_moments.stakes: Imminent armed encounter; protecting interview subjects from reprisal

peak_moments.description: Ruby Hurley clandestinely organizing in banned states
Clarence Norris asking the NAACP to clear his name before he dies
peak_moments.stakes: Norris as a fugitive seeking exoneration after decades

conflict_types: Organizational defiance of state authority (membership rolls)
Inter-organizational disagreement (NAACP vs Communist Party in original Scottsboro)
tension_conflict_triumph.emotional_range: From childhood fear (segment 53) to executive composure (segment 297) to elder reflection on fear that 'comes after' (segment 476)
tension_conflict_triumph.justification: Several genuine high-stakes moments are described concretely. Roxborough's executive restraint means the tension is recounted rather than relived; the narrative arc is one of dignified survival rather than dramatic intensity.

universal_themes: Childhood pride at recognition from an admired figure
Fear and the management of fear
relatable_struggles: Being the young new employee thrown into a difficult assignment
Traveling alone and managing personal safety
human_moments: Receiving the typed letter from Roy Wilkins at age nine (Segment 47, 00:05:41,000)
Refusing to mislead history with secondhand Du Bois stories (Segment 433, 01:14:24,000)
Demurring from naming her proudest accomplishment (Segments 437-470)
relatable_experiences.connection_points: Audiences who have worked in any long-tenured institutional role will recognize her arc; readers attuned to women's professional history will find a model of dignified, competent advancement.
relatable_experiences.justification: The interview reaches universal themes through her childhood pride, family inheritance, and managed fear. The professional-executive framing makes some passages context-specific and less immediately accessible to general audiences without civil-rights-movement background.

emotional_resonance.overall_assessment: Emotionally resonant in a measured, executive key. The most powerful moments -- the shotgun, the father's beating, the Crisis letter, the Norris walk-in -- are vivid scenes that work as standalone illustrations of stakes. Roxborough's reserve is itself emotionally meaningful: she models how Movement institutional workers maintained dignity under pressure.

barriers: Frequent NAACP-specific acronyms and titles (LDF, ACT-SO, Department of Branches, General Counsel) used without explanation
Whisper-transcribed speech in places renders unfamiliar -- e.g., 'O-fokes' for 'old folks' (segment 240), 'Snake' for 'SNCC' (segment 239)
language_clarity.justification: Roxborough is articulate, precise, and self-corrects ('I'm sorry, 1954'). Her vocabulary is professional-managerial without affectation. The acronym-heavy NAACP organizational vocabulary will challenge listeners unfamiliar with the institution.

high_energy_sections: The Norris walk-in and pardon narrative (Segments 122-176, 00:24:35,000 - 00:29:34,000)
The Mississippi Delta shotgun and Medgar Evers field work (Segments 318-330)
The succession of personality portraits Marshall/Wilkins/White (Segments 64-86)
slow_sections: Some passages of organizational commentary on peer-org comparison feel abstract relative to the personality-portrait sections (Segments 234-252)
The interviewer-driven topic shifts produce some choppiness across the second half of the interview
narrative_flow.attention_sustainability: Sustained for engaged listeners through about 60-75 minutes; the interviewer's frequent topic shifts and a tape change late in the interview slightly fragment attention.
narrative_flow.justification: Roxborough's own pacing is steady and assured; the interview's rhythm is more affected by the interviewer's question style than by her delivery.

themes_connected: NAACP's institutional role in 20th-century American civil rights
Generational transmission of activism in Black families
Women's under-recognized labor in social movements
Litigation vs. legislation as theories of social change
Behind-the-scenes work as foundation for public victories
broader_themes_connection.contemporary_relevance: Roxborough's frame -- that the public forgets historical struggles and takes inherited rights for granted -- connects directly to current debates about civil-rights backlash and the fragility of institutional gains. Her account of NAACP membership-roll defiance is relevant to contemporary state-pressure-on-civil-society debates.
broader_themes_connection.meaning_making: Roxborough actively interprets her own work in broader terms, explicitly connecting NAACP-branch organizing to enduring community trust ('people have felt that they could get help going to the NAACP').
broader_themes_connection.justification: Roxborough is unusually deliberate about thematic framing -- she steps back from anecdote to articulate broader institutional meaning, which gives her interview strong thematic accessibility for general audiences willing to follow her organizational vocabulary.

accessibility.overall_assessment: Generally accessible to engaged listeners; the dominant accessibility limit is the assumed knowledge of NAACP organizational structure and vocabulary. Her clear articulation and explicit thematic framing partly offset this.

engagement_scores.overall_assessment: This is a high-historical-value interview with very-good engagement quality. Roxborough's 44-year NAACP National Office tenure, her direct contact with virtually every major NAACP figure of the mid-20th century, and her precise, historian-trustworthy recall make this an indispensable institutional-history source. Emotional and narrative resonance are measured -- characteristic of an executive of her generation -- but several vivid scenes (1939 beating, 1955 Crisis letter, shotgun-in-the-car) anchor the interview memorably. Accessibility is limited primarily by the assumed familiarity with NAACP organizational vocabulary.
key_strengths: Exceptional firsthand-witness range: 44 years on NAACP National staff with direct contact with Walter White, Roy Wilkins, Thurgood Marshall, Benjamin Hooks, Rosa Parks, Medgar Evers, Daisy Bates, Mamie Till Bradley, Clarence Norris, and more.

Rare focus on women's leadership pipeline within the NAACP (Lampkin, Black, Branch, Shagaloff, Motley, Simmons, Hooks, Wilson, Brock) and on the under-recognized legislative-lobbying dimension of the NAACP's civil-rights record.

Methodologically careful recall: explicit self-corrections ('1955 -- I'm sorry, 1954'), refusal to repeat secondhand Du Bois stories 'to mislead the history,' and steady distinction between speaker observation and second-hand report.
key_limitations: Executive-style emotional reserve limits the deeply personal vulnerability that the highest-engagement testimonies achieve; significant feelings are described rather than re-experienced.
Assumed NAACP organizational vocabulary (department names, executive titles, acronyms) creates an accessibility bar for general audiences unfamiliar with the institution.

claims.claim: Mildred Bond Roxborough joined the NAACP national staff in 1953 and continued in a full-time role through 1997.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I came, started work in the fall of 1953 to do a special project for Gloster B. Current, Director of Branches (Segment 63). The reason I'm still probably at the NAACP in one sense, I worked as a full-time person until 1997 (Segment 441).
claims.rationale: Both years explicitly stated by the speaker.

claims.claim: Roxborough was born in Brownsville, Haywood County, Tennessee, where Black residents could not vote and outnumbered white residents three to one.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Haywood County, Tennessee was the county in which we lived. The county seat was Brownsville in which I was born (Segments 18-19). And they felt that it was important to organize our black citizens because they outnumbered the white citizens by three to one (Segment 20).
claims.rationale: All elements explicitly stated.

claims.claim: Her parents were teachers who organized one of the early NAACP branches in Haywood County, with her father as branch president and mother as branch secretary.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Well, by 1939, the branch had been organized. He was the president, my mother was secretary (Segment 49). Well, well, my parents were teachers (Segment 33).
claims.rationale: Stated directly.

claims.claim: As a young child Roxborough read The Crisis magazine on her mother's lap and wrote to Roy Wilkins, then editor of The Crisis, asking permission to sell subscriptions.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And I would be sitting on my mother's lap. And oftentimes in reading poetry, she would quote to them saying that this is a piece by Langston Hughes, which was published in The Crisis magazine (Segment 35). Can I write to Roy Wilkins, who was the editor of The Crisis magazine? And I asked him, if I can't sell The Crisis magazine (Segment 41).
claims.rationale: Directly supported. Note: ground-truth corpus confirms Roy Wilkins took over The Crisis from W.E.B. Du Bois in 1934, consistent with this timeline.

claims.claim: Roxborough's father was beaten by law-enforcement officials and brought home in 1939 when she was about nine.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Now, can you share any details with us about what happened to your father as a result of his civil rights activities in 1939? Oh, well, by 1939... He was usually let go. However, one evening, he was brought home having been rather badly beaten (Segments 48-50). I was about nine at the time (Segment 53).
claims.rationale: 1939 named by interviewer, confirmed by Roxborough; her age stated directly.

claims.claim: Dr. John Severe, the white county coroner of Haywood County and her grandmother's half-brother, intervened on her father's behalf.
claims.supporting_excerpt: the county coroner of Haywood County, Tennessee. His name was John Severe was a doctor. And she said, Doctor Severe probably asked them not to do anything to your father, because he, he's your grandmother's brother... it was half brother, because he was white (Segments 54-55).
claims.rationale: Speaker provides all details directly.

claims.claim: Roxborough attended Howard University from 1943 to 1945 during World War II.
claims.supporting_excerpt: This is 43, 1943. And from 43 to 45, I was at Howard. And of course, we were also concerned about our studies (Segment 60).
claims.rationale: Directly stated.

claims.claim: Director of Branches Gloster B. Current sent her to Baltimore for a six-week membership campaign under branch president Dr. Lillie M. Jackson.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I came, started work in the fall of 1953 to do a special project for Gloster B. Current, Director of Branches in Baltimore, Maryland (Segment 63). It also had as its president, Dr. Lillie M. Jackson (Segment 64). I was going to Baltimore to do a six week membership campaign for the Baltimore branch (Segment 65).
claims.rationale: Every claim directly transcribed.

claims.claim: Jackson's daughter Juanita Jackson Mitchell was the first organizer of the National NAACP Youth Program.
claims.supporting_excerpt: her daughter Juanita Jackson Mitchell, by that time, was a first organizer of the Youth Councils, of the Youth Program for the National NAACP (Segment 68).
claims.rationale: Direct.

claims.claim: Henry G. Parks Jr. was president of Parks Sausage Company, an early Black corporation.
claims.supporting_excerpt: there was a man who was the president of Parks Sausage Company, which was an early black corporation, which was, if not the first of his kind, was one of the first (Segment 69). So that had been Henry Parks? Yes, Henry G. Parks Jr (Segment 72).
claims.rationale: Directly transcribed.

claims.claim: 'Little Willie' Adams supported the Baltimore branch despite income reportedly from numbers operations.
claims.supporting_excerpt: one of the great fundus was a locally known man, well known man who was his income, supposedly it was derived from numbers and that type of thing (Segment 70). the man was Little Willie Adams (Segment 72).
claims.rationale: Directly transcribed.

claims.claim: Thurgood Marshall reacted to her assignment by laughing and telling Current, 'Gloucester, why are you doing that to that girl? You should be ashamed of yourself.'
claims.supporting_excerpt: Thurgood Marshall who was sitting a couple of seats away from me laughed loudly and said, Gloucester, why are you doing that to that girl? You should be ashamed of yourself (Segment 66).
claims.rationale: Direct quote in transcript.

claims.claim: Roxborough describes Roy Wilkins as elegant, attentive to language, unpretentious, riding the subway to work.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Very elegant man. One who was extremely... concerned about the language and its use and the syntax... And he wrote the subway, which at that time was five cents going and coming to work (Segments 78-79).
claims.rationale: Directly described. Whisper renders 'rode the subway' as 'wrote the subway' — context-clear transcription artifact.

claims.claim: Roxborough describes Walter White as so fair-skinned he could 'presume to be Caucasian,' enabling lynching investigations in Mississippi, Georgia, and Arkansas.
claims.supporting_excerpt: And he would presume to be Caucasian unless you would know otherwise. So he was successful in going into places like Mississippi, Georgia and Arkansas and investigating lunches and other incidents (Segments 84-85).
claims.rationale: Speaker statement; note 'lunches' is a Whisper artifact for 'lynchings.'

claims.claim: Roxborough first met Daisy Bates in 1954 in Hoxie, Arkansas during the lead-up to Central High integration, when Orval Faubus was governor.
claims.supporting_excerpt: this is in the 50s now. This was just before 56. This was 55, 1955 when I first went, I'm sorry, 1954 when I first went to Little Rock (Segment 90). The governor of Arkansas was Orval Faubus (Segment 89). A moment ago you mentioned Hoxie, Arkansas and that took you into contact with Daisy Bates (Segment 87).
claims.rationale: Speaker self-corrects to 1954. Faubus and Hoxie context directly stated.

claims.claim: Roxborough stayed in Rosa Parks's Montgomery home before Parks left Alabama, and Parks was then secretary of the Montgomery NAACP branch.
claims.supporting_excerpt: once when I first went to Montgomery, I stayed with Mrs. Parks. This is before, of course, she left Alabama (Segment 96). at that point, she was still secretary of the branch (Segment 98).
claims.rationale: Both elements directly stated. Ground-truth corpus confirms Parks was Montgomery NAACP secretary from 1943.

claims.claim: E.D. Nixon refused to give up NAACP membership rolls when the Alabama Attorney General demanded them, after which the NAACP was banned from operating in Alabama.
claims.supporting_excerpt: he refused to give up the membership roles of the NAACP in Alabama when the Attorney General demanded it. And then after that period, you may recall that the NAACP was banned from operating in Alabama (Segments 103-104).
claims.rationale: Directly stated. Ground-truth corpus confirms NAACP v. Alabama (1958) involved the protection of NAACP membership lists from state government compulsion.

claims.claim: Emmett Till's murder came shortly after the killing of NAACP branch president Gus Courts in Mississippi.
claims.supporting_excerpt: It was considered in our office to be a great tragedy because just before then, and I'm sorry, I can't remember the town, one of our branch presidents had been killed in Mississippi. Gus Courts. I think, yeah (Segments 107-108).
claims.rationale: Speaker names Gus Courts in close temporal proximity to Till and identifies him as a Mississippi branch president. Note: historical record establishes Gus Courts as a Belzoni MS NAACP officer who was shot but survived (the better-known 1955 Mississippi NAACP-officer murder was George W. Lee, also of Belzoni). Roxborough's claim that Courts was killed is the speaker's recollection but may be a memory slip (Courts survived being shot in November 1955). Marked partially_supported because the speaker's framing of 'killed' may not match the historical record.

claims.claim: Roxborough arranged and partially accompanied a national NAACP-branch tour for Emmett Till's mother Mamie Till Bradley.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Yes, I arranged a tour, I guess we could, of NAACP branches, for Mrs. Bradley to visit. And actually, a company in her for some of the time, to some of the cities, which we arranged for her to visit (Segments 114-115).
claims.rationale: Directly stated.

claims.claim: Clarence Norris, the last surviving Scottsboro defendant, walked into the NAACP's 1790 Broadway office in the late 1960s seeking help.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Our office moved to 1790 Broadway off Columbus Circle. And one day this man came into the office... it developed his name as Clarence Norris (Segments 122-124). And this had to be, I guess, in the late 60s when this happened (Segment 134).
claims.rationale: All elements directly stated.

claims.claim: General Counsel Nathaniel R. Jones assigned attorney James I. Meyerson to pursue Norris's pardon, which was ultimately granted in Alabama.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Roy Wilkins, that spoke to Nathaniel R. Jones, who was our General Counsel at that point... And he assigned one of the bright young attorneys whose name was James I. Meyerson to work on the issue of getting a full pardon for Willie Norris (Segments 169-173). Myerson and Wilkins and Jones and Norris, go to Alabama and secure his name. Meet the governor and the attorney general and get a full pardon (Segments 174-175).
claims.rationale: Directly stated. Transcript intermittently renders 'Clarence' as 'Willie' (Whisper artifact). The historical record establishes Clarence Norris received a full pardon from Alabama Governor George Wallace on October 25, 1976, with NAACP attorney James Meyerson representing him -- consistent with the speaker's account.

claims.claim: ACT-SO was founded by Benjamin L. Hooks and Vernon Jarrett, a newspaper reporter from Chicago.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Now this program was founded by Benjamin L. Hooks and Vernon Jarrett, who was a newspaper reporter from Chicago (Segment 190).
claims.rationale: Direct statement.

claims.claim: Walter White died in 1955 while on leave, and Roy Wilkins -- who had been serving as administrator -- was named his successor at the next board meeting.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Walter White died in 1955. Suddenly, he had been ill and on leave, but Roy Wilkins had been serving in... He had a leave of Apsons, Walter White did. Right. And Roy Wilkins had been serving as administrator (Segments 206-210). after the Serp funeral, of course, at the next board meeting, I believe it was, in the spring board meeting, was named as his successor (Segment 211).
claims.rationale: Directly stated. Ground-truth corpus confirms Wilkins served as executive secretary 1955-1964.

claims.claim: Roxborough characterizes James Farmer as having 'formerly worked for the NAACP as our program director' and as the leader of CORE.
claims.supporting_excerpt: The Corps was a little more intellectual and it had an excellent leader in James Farmer who formerly worked for the NAACP as our program director (Segment 245).
claims.rationale: Speaker statement. Ground-truth corpus establishes Farmer was a CORE co-founder and national director 1961-1966; the corpus does not specifically discuss his NAACP role but the speaker's claim is internally credible and not contradicted by available facts.

claims.claim: Roxborough attributes Executive Order 8802 (the Fair Employment Practices Committee) to NAACP lobbying, facilitated by Eleanor Roosevelt's role on the NAACP Board and Walter White's collaboration with A. Philip Randolph.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Executive Order 8802... was done as a result of the NAACP's lobbying and campaigning. And the fact that Mrs. Roosevelt was on our board of directors, Walter White and Mrs. Roosevelt had a good relationship, and it was possible to get Mrs. Roosevelt to work with Walter White and Philip Randolph and get the President, then Roosevelt, to understand that an executive order, to desegregating federal installations, should be issued (Segments 258-260).
claims.rationale: Directly stated. Ground-truth corpus confirms A. Philip Randolph led the 1941 March on Washington Movement that pressured FDR to issue EO 8802 and create the FEPC; the corpus describes this as Randolph-led, which is consistent with the speaker's multi-party attribution (NAACP + Randolph).

claims.claim: General Counsel Robert L. Carter made some 36 appearances before the Supreme Court and won about 30 of those cases.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Form of General Counsel Robert Carter, it made some 36 appearances before the Supreme Court, and won about 30 of those cases (Segment 264).
claims.rationale: Speaker provides specific numbers; ground-truth corpus confirms Robert L. Carter served as NAACP General Counsel 1956-1968 and argued multiple landmark Supreme Court cases, but does not give a precise lifetime SCOTUS count for Carter. The exact 36/30 figures are the speaker's recollection and not independently verified here.

claims.claim: Roxborough's late husband John Roxborough was Chairman of the Legal Redress Committee of the Detroit NAACP branch and lead lawyer in Michigan Supreme Court cases involving housing and education.
claims.supporting_excerpt: before I knew him, John, he was chairman of the Legal and Redress Committee of Detroit branch, having been born in Michigan and reared in Michigan (Segment 279). they had won two or three cases in the Michigan before the Michigan Supreme Court involving housing and education. And John was the lead lawyer in those particular cases (Segments 281-282).
claims.rationale: Directly stated.

claims.claim: After the NAACP was banned in Alabama, Ruby Hurley relocated her Southeast Region office from Birmingham to Atlanta and continued clandestine organizing in banned states.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Mrs. Ruby Hurley, who was sent to Alabama to organize units of the NAACP in the southeast region... headquartered in Birmingham... She moved after the NAACP was banned. The office there was moved to Atlanta (Segments 300-302). Mrs. Hurley and other volunteers went almost sort of ticiously into communities to work again (Segment 306).
claims.rationale: Directly stated.

claims.claim: Following Medgar Evers's assassination, his brother Charles took over the Mississippi field secretary position.
claims.supporting_excerpt: actually his brother took the position, took over the position of field director for the state of Mississippi (Segment 314).
claims.rationale: Directly stated. Ground-truth corpus confirms Charles Evers succeeded Medgar Evers as Mississippi Field Secretary (later Director) from 1963 to 1987.

claims.claim: Roxborough traveled with Medgar Evers in the Mississippi Delta interviewing farmers denied credit because of NAACP activity, including a night drive where she found her bare feet were resting on his shotgun.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Medgar Evers and I as a field director, he would drive to various places... we went into the Delta area to get an interview farmer (Segments 319-320). I had taken off my shoes and I felt something on the floor which was cold. And I said to them, what is this kid, maybe I can move it. He said, well, that's my shotgun, you have your feet on (Segments 326-327).
claims.rationale: All elements directly stated. Setting (Mound Bayou, Yazoo) supported by ground-truth corpus entry on Mound Bayou as a Black town with operational base in 1950s-60s Mississippi Delta civil rights work.

claims.claim: Roxborough specifically remembers Ernest Green for his 'quiet determination' (Green later became an NAACP Board member) and Minnijean Brown for her 'special, sturdy personality.'
claims.supporting_excerpt: But the thing that impressed me was one, the quiet determination of Ernest Green. I remembered him who later became a member of the NACP Board of Directors. And another one who I remember is Minnijean Brown, who had a special 30 personality (Segments 353-355).
claims.rationale: All elements stated. Whisper renders 'sturdy' as '30' — context-clear artifact.

claims.claim: Ella Baker worked at the NAACP's Department of Branches before Roxborough joined and was later involved in the founding of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee.
claims.supporting_excerpt: She worked in our Department of Branches before I came on board to the NAACP. And she spent considerable time traveling around the country, organizing branches (Segments 371-372). she became focused on our youth, if you recall, and actually was involved in the founding of the Student Nonviolent Coordinating Committee (Segment 370).
claims.rationale: Directly stated. Ground-truth corpus confirms Ella Baker's 1960 SNCC founding role at Shaw University.

claims.claim: Roxborough briefly corresponded with W.E.B. Du Bois, who replied agreeing to send a copy of one of his papers for five dollars.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I met him, but and and had written him for a copy of one of his documents, one of his papers. And he wrote back saying he'd be delighted to send it to send $5. So, I sent the $5 and got the paper (Segment 429).
claims.rationale: Directly stated.

claims.claim: Roxborough rotated through 'almost every department in the association except its accounting department,' eventually serving as Director of Operations, Director of Programs, and Development Director.
claims.supporting_excerpt: I have held, see, probably about eight or ten jobs and almost every department in the association except it's accounting department (Segment 442). I would then go from, I was a director of operations at one point, director of programs. I wound up being a development director (Segments 457-458).
claims.rationale: Directly stated.

claims.claim: Margaret Bush Wilson and Roslyn M. Brock both served as Chairs of the NAACP Board of Directors.
claims.supporting_excerpt: You wound up having Margaret Bush Wilson + Roslyn M. Brock become the second female to be chairman of the board of the NAACP. And then Vice Chair has developed Roslyn M. Brock, who has succeeded you as Chair (Segments 419-420).
claims.rationale: Both names directly stated. Note: the Whisper rendering of the chairs-sequence claim is muddled; the summary preserves the claim that both held leadership without claiming the specific ordinal.

claims.claim: Frances Hooks, the wife of Benjamin Hooks, founded Women in the NAACP.
claims.supporting_excerpt: Mrs. Francis Hooke's, the wife of Benjamin Hooke's, founded when women in the NAACP, which gave them a structure in which to function (Segment 418).
claims.rationale: Directly stated. Whisper renders 'Frances' as 'Francis' — context-clear artifact.

claims.claim: Constance Baker Motley worked in the NAACP Legal Department and later moved to a leadership role in the NAACP Legal Defense Fund.

publication_decision.rationale: The main summary's accuracy score is 88 (below the 90 threshold), driven by Component B caution about preserving the speaker's voice without correcting transcript-period framings (e.g., the Charles Evers 'mayor of Philadelphia' speaker slip and the 1919 silent-march date that historical record places in 1917). The summary's quality score is exactly 90, and the citation audit records 37 of 38 claims as fully supported with 1 partially supported (the Gus Courts 'killed' claim, which the historical record more accurately documents as a non-fatal shooting). The combination of accuracy < 90 AND one partially_supported citation routes this output to human review per the dual-scoring + citation-audit gate. Human review should specifically check: (1) whether to retain the speaker's 'killed' framing of Gus Courts vs. add a clarifying historical footnote; (2) whether to retain or correct the silent-march date framing; (3) whether the Charles Evers Philadelphia/Fayette city should be addressed in editorial notes. The chapter-level summaries are uniformly stronger (most above 90 on both axes) and the engagement score is solidly in the 'Very Good' band; the human-review block is a conservative call on main-summary fidelity rather than a signal of poor underlying quality.
